quality.rationale: 热力学恒等式推导+多步代数运算+相对论气体模型

origin.question:
Problem 5.34. The invariant equilibrium distribution function of a relativistic gas is (25-1) $ rac{1}{4}$ .
$$
\mathcal {N} \left(\mathrm {p}^{\alpha}, \mathrm {x}^{\alpha}\right) \equiv \frac {\mathrm {d N}}{\mathrm {d}^{3} \mathrm {x d}^{3} \mathrm {P}} = \frac {(2 \mathrm {J} + 1) / \mathrm {h}^{3}}{\exp \left[ - \frac {\mathrm {P} \cdot \mathrm {u}}{\mathrm {k T}} - \theta \right] - \varepsilon}.
$$
Here $\mathbf{J} =$ spin of particles, $\mathbf{h} =$ Planck's constant, $\mathbf{u} =$ mean 4-velocity of gas, $\varepsilon = 1,0$ or $-1$ for Bose-Einstein, Maxwell-Boltzmann or Fermi-Dirac statistics respectively. The parameter $	heta$ is independent of $\mathbf{P}$ . The first two moments of $\mathfrak{N}$ are
$$
\mathrm {J}^{\mu} \equiv \int \mathcal {N} \mathrm {P}^{\mu} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{3} \mathrm {P}}{(- \mathrm {P} \cdot \mathrm {u})}, \qquad \mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \equiv \int \mathcal {N} \mathrm {P}^{\mu} \mathrm {P}^{\nu} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{3} \mathrm {P}}{(- \mathrm {P} \cdot \mathrm {u})}.
$$
Since $\mathbf{u}$ is the only free vector, these integrals must have the form
$$
J^{\mu} = n u^{\mu}, \quad T^{\mu \nu} = (\rho + p) u^{\mu} u^{\nu} + p g^{\mu \nu}.
$$
(This is the kinetic-theory definition of $\mathfrak{n},\rho ,\mathfrak{p}$ .)
(c) Use the first law of thermodynamics to identify $\mathrm{kT}\theta$ as the chemical potential $\mu = (\rho +\mathfrak{p}) / \mathfrak{n} - \mathfrak{T}\mathfrak{s}$ .
origin.solution:
(c) From the definition of $\mu$ we have
$$
\mathrm {d} \mu = \mathrm {d} \rho / \mathrm {n} + \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d n} / \mathrm {n}^{2} - \mathrm {s d T} - \mathrm {T d s},
$$
but
$$
\mathrm {d} \rho = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d n} / \mathrm {n} + \mathrm {n T d s},
$$
therefore
$$
\mathrm {d} \mu = \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - \mathrm {s d T} = \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d T} / (\mathrm {n T}) + \mu \mathrm {d T} / \mathrm {T}.
$$
Here we have substituted for $s$ in terms of $\mu$ . Comparing this with Equation (4), we identify
$$
\mu = k T \theta .
$$
